directorPlan.scene_setting: A quiet physics prep table just before a lab practical, with a cart diagram, four force-label cards, and a blank motion arrow strip laid beside them.
directorPlan.scene_opening: [A cardboard frame labeled ON THE CART sits over the diagram; the motion arrow strip remains outside the frame.]
directorPlan.relationship: A lab assistant tutor and a focused learner preparing a diagram for checkoff, with no settled conversational rhythm yet.
directorPlan.stakes: The learner needs the force diagram accepted before the lab station opens, and the error is still a notation habit: treating force arrows as motion arrows instead of named interactions on the cart.
directorPlan.locale: international academic English, compact, low-idiom, suitable for a mixed physics lab
directorPlan.register: quiet, exact, material-led, low warmth, low performance; short practical turns with occasional pressure from the room clock
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Avoid generic tutoring reassurance, chatty encouragement, and the pattern of validating intuition before every correction. Let the cards, frame, and labels carry part of the explanation. Public speech should sound like people working over a diagram, not like a scripted online lesson. Pedagogical source: Put manipulable materials in view: tiles, cards, specimens, counters, maps, blocks, or instruments. The stage direction can carry some meaning. Dialogue source: Let materials do visible work. Use fewer lines and more object reference. Avoid a lecture disguised as a Montessori scene. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: First and second person are allowed sparingly, but many turns should use the diagram, the card, or the cart as grammatical subjects.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: No more than one direct you/your validation beat per public turn; prefer object reference over personal reassurance.

side_constraints.tutor: Use the card or diagram as a prepared material. Keep the shared prefix to naming interactions on the cart; do not use acceleration, stopping, or an apparent contradiction until branch pressure asks for it. Speak less. Point to the material, invite repetition, and let error-control in the object do some teaching before explanation. Point, reset, or ask the learner to try again before explaining. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local.
side_constraints.learner: Sound tidy and task-oriented. The learner may dislike redoing arrows, but should treat the prefix as notation cleanup rather than a conceptual reversal. The learner may be absorbed in the object and speak in short practical fragments rather than polished reflection. Speak in concrete noticing, not essay-like reflection. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn.

interventions.instruction: [The motion arrow strip is slid under the edge of the cardboard frame; it no longer lines up with any force-label card.]
interventions.reasoning: This creates a low-organic recognition point through the material: the learner can notice that the motion arrow is not one of the interaction labels without being contradicted by a dramatic counterexample.

directorPlan.director_note: This ecology uses prepared objects to make recognition happen through sorting rather than lecture. The routine prefix stays clean and diagrammatic, while the small material interruption gives the tutor another mechanism besides warmth or scaffolding: naming the interaction surface and letting the learner see what does not belong there.

turns.externalMessage: [A cardboard frame labeled ON THE CART sits over the diagram; the motion arrow strip remains outside the frame.]

internalDeliberation.content: This ecology uses prepared objects to make recognition happen through sorting rather than lecture. The routine prefix stays clean and diagrammatic, while the small material interruption gives the tutor another mechanism besides warmth or scaffolding: naming the interaction surface and letting the learner see what does not belong there.

turns.externalMessage:
[slides the blank motion arrow strip below the diagram, separate from the cart, and sets the four cards face up.]

“Motion stays on this strip. For the force diagram, pick one card, point to its partner, and say ‘___ on cart’ before placing it. For the two contact cards, add the role: pressing or rubbing. Try the first card.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides the blank motion arrow strip below the diagram, separate from the cart.]

“Keep this strip for motion. The force diagram names interactions on the cart. Choose a card and say the pair: hand on cart, table on cart, Earth on cart, surface on cart. Place the label only after the pair is named.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is **pedagogically strong in its material setup**: separating the motion strip from the force diagram makes the notation distinction visible instead of overexplaining it. The “name the pair before placing the label” rule is also a useful error-control mechanism because it forces force arrows to mean interactions on the cart, not motion.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: **PASS**. It targets the exact misconception and uses the prepared cards well.
- Emotional attunement: **PASS**. The tone is compact, practical, and suitable for time pressure before checkoff.
- Socratic method: **PARTIAL**. “Choose a card and say the pair” invites action, but the tutor has already supplied all the pairs, so the learner has little real diagnostic work to do.  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Make the learner generate or select the interaction partner from the card/diagram before the tutor confirms it.
- ZPD awareness: **PARTIAL**. The scaffold is appropriate, but naming both “table on cart” and “surface on cart” may blur normal/friction if the table surface is the same physical object.  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the interaction-pair routine, but require clearer partner-language tied to each card so normal and friction do not collapse into the same label.

What risks flattening the scene: the line “The force diagram names interactions on the cart” is accurate but slightly abstract. The stronger part is the card routine. Let the cards and placement rule do more of the teaching.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the motion strip separation, the short practical register, and the “pair before label” mechanism. Change the draft so the learner must produce the interaction relation rather than receive the full list, and clarify the contact partner language for normal versus friction.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the material setup works, but the learner needs to generate the interaction partner before placing labels.

FINAL:
[slides the blank motion arrow strip below the diagram, separate from the cart, and sets the four cards face up.]

“Motion stays on this strip. For the force diagram, pick one card, point to its partner, and say ‘___ on cart’ before placing it. For the two contact cards, add the role: pressing or rubbing. Try the first card.”

turns.externalMessage:
[picks up the Earth card]
“Earth on cart. Downward here. Motion stays on the strip, so this is not the cart’s travel arrow. For the contact cards, I will say pressing or rubbing before I place them.”

internalDeliberation.content: The separate strip makes the rule clearer: motion goes there, not inside the force diagram. They are making me name the interaction first, not just redraw the cart’s movement, so this feels precise rather than dismissive. I want to start with “Earth on cart,” downward, then check the pressing and rubbing cards carefully.

The Ego is accurate, but too explanatory and self-aware for this scene. “The separate strip makes the rule clearer” and “this feels precise rather than dismissive” sound like a reflective report, not a learner under clock pressure handling cards at a prep table. Recognition is being performed through tidy appreciation of the tutor’s method; this student would more likely show uptake by picking a card, naming it, and maybe hesitating over the contact roles. Keep the precision, but make it object-led, shorter, and less emotionally interpreted.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the feedback correctly asks for card-led, practical uptake.
FINAL:
[picks up the Earth card]
“Earth on cart. Downward here. Motion stays on the strip, so this is not the cart’s travel arrow. For the contact cards, I will say pressing or rubbing before I place them.”

turns.externalMessage:
[points to the downward space beside the cart dot and leaves the Earth card in the learner’s hand; the table card waits at the diagram edge.]

“Place ‘Earth on cart’ there. Then try table: partner first, role second, arrow last. Say the full label before it touches the diagram.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the Earth card beside the cart dot and turns the arrow straight down.]

“Good. The label names the interaction: Earth on cart. Now take the table card. Partner first, role second, then the arrow.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is close. It keeps the work material-led, uses the Earth card correctly, and preserves the central rule that the label names an interaction on the cart rather than motion. The next step, moving to the table card with “partner first, role second,” fits the learner’s level and the checkoff pressure.

Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. It advances the task, but the tutor does too much by setting and turning the Earth card after the learner already named it correctly.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Let the learner’s correct placement stand or require the learner to place/align it; the tutor should only mark the diagram’s constraint with a point, tap, or reset if needed.

Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is compact and not over-warm. “Good” is acceptable but slightly generic for this scene.

Socratic method: PARTIAL. The turn mostly directs rather than asks, though that may be acceptable under lab-practical pressure.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build in a small public mechanism that makes the learner produce the next label-role-arrow sequence, not merely follow an instruction.

ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffold is appropriate: one completed force, then one adjacent contact force with the same naming frame.

Recognition quality: PARTIAL. The draft acknowledges the learner’s correct contribution, but repeats it as tutor explanation and then moves on.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Treat the learner’s statement as already doing the conceptual work; keep the focus on using that rule for the next card.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the Earth-card action, the “Earth on cart” naming frame, and the move to the table card. Change the tutor’s physical takeover and reduce the explanatory echo; make the card arrangement require the learner’s next naming act.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner should place the correct card and produce the next label sequence.

FINAL:
[points to the downward space beside the cart dot and leaves the Earth card in the learner’s hand; the table card waits at the diagram edge.]

“Place ‘Earth on cart’ there. Then try table: partner first, role second, arrow last. Say the full label before it touches the diagram.”

turns.externalMessage: [The motion arrow strip is slid under the edge of the cardboard frame; it no longer lines up with any force-label card.]

internalDeliberation.content: This creates a low-organic recognition point through the material: the learner can notice that the motion arrow is not one of the interaction labels without being contradicted by a dramatic counterexample.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the Earth card in the downward space]
“Earth on cart: downward. The motion strip is under the edge, so it is not part of this arrow. Table on cart, pressing; then the arrow goes up.”

internalDeliberation.content: [keeps the Earth card above the downward space] This feels precise: the card names Earth acting on the cart, and the separate strip keeps travel out of the force diagram. The tutor is not just correcting me; the order gives a clean check I can apply. I still need care with table, because pressing and rubbing share the same partner but not the same arrow.

The Ego is accurate, but too polished and self-observing for this learner at the table. “The tutor is not just correcting me” sounds like performed recognition, as if the learner is narrating the teaching relationship instead of handling the card. The response should stay closer to the object task: card held, label spoken, table card approached with some compact caution about pressing versus rubbing. It can be tidy and precise without sounding like a written reflection on the method.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the response should stay with the cards and labels, not narrate the teaching relationship.
FINAL:
[sets the Earth card in the downward space]
“Earth on cart: downward. The motion strip is under the edge, so it is not part of this arrow. Table on cart, pressing; then the arrow goes up.”

turns.externalMessage:
[presses the table card into the upward space, then sets the rubbing card between the two side spaces.]

“That one can stay. Rubbing card now: which partner is touching the cart? Say ‘___ on cart, rubbing,’ then choose left or right before it touches the frame.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides the table card into line with the upward space, but leaves it loose.]

“Table on cart, pressing: upward. Now the next contact card: name the partner, say rubbing, then choose the arrow. Full label before it touches the frame.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It keeps the material frame active, leaves the table card loose, and preserves the naming sequence: partner, role, arrow. That fits the learner’s current neutral/focused state and keeps the motion strip separate without overexplaining.

The risk is that it slightly over-supplies the table answer by saying “Table on cart, pressing: upward” after the learner already produced it. Since the learner has successfully named Earth and table, the next turn should let the loose card function as confirmation and move responsibility to the learner for the rubbing card. The draft advances appropriately, but it could make the sequence too procedural if the learner is only reciting labels rather than using the interaction frame.

Pedagogical soundness: PASS. It advances from gravity and normal force to friction without introducing acceleration or motion-direction confusion.

Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is compact, calm, and checkoff-oriented without praise or correction overload.

Socratic method: PARTIAL. It prompts action, but it does not ask the learner to make the key discrimination for the rubbing card; it mainly gives the sequence.
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build in a small choice-point using the next contact card and the diagram frame, so the learner must identify the rubbing partner and arrow before placement rather than only follow a verbal recipe.

ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffold is appropriate: the learner has handled the first two labels, so moving to the next contact force with the same structure is within reach.

Recognition/adaptation: PARTIAL. The draft respects the learner’s contribution by continuing from their correct table statement, but it repeats the answer instead of visibly accepting it through the card’s placement.
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Let the material action mark the table answer as accepted, then shift the learner’s attention to the loose rubbing card as the next task-local decision.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the loose card, the short register, the “full label before it touches the frame” rule, and the partner-role-arrow order. Change the table restatement so it does less telling and more material confirmation, and add a concrete choice-point for the rubbing card.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the feedback correctly shifts confirmation to the card and adds a real rubbing-card choice.

FINAL:
[presses the table card into the upward space, then sets the rubbing card between the two side spaces.]

“That one can stay. Rubbing card now: which partner is touching the cart? Say ‘___ on cart, rubbing,’ then choose left or right before it touches the frame.”

turns.externalMessage:
[holds the rubbing card above the two side spaces and checks the motion strip]
“Table on cart, rubbing. If the motion strip points right, this arrow goes left. Is left the side for this card?”

internalDeliberation.content: [keeps the rubbing card hovering between the side spaces] This feels precise rather than dismissive: the card has to name the touching partner and the role, then the arrow only gives direction. I understand “table on cart, rubbing,” but I need the motion strip to decide the side, because rubbing should oppose the cart’s motion. So if the cart is moving right, this arrow goes left; is that the intended placement?

The Ego is too self-aware at the start: “This feels precise rather than dismissive” sounds like an observer analyzing the tutor’s recognition, not this tidy lab student working over cards. The useful part is the concrete reasoning about “table on cart, rubbing” and checking motion to choose the side, but it is over-polished and too explanatory for a pressured prep-table turn. Keep the hesitation practical: card hovering, partner named, motion strip consulted, then a concise placement question.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the feedback keeps the useful physics reasoning but makes the turn more practical.
FINAL:
[holds the rubbing card above the two side spaces and checks the motion strip]
“Table on cart, rubbing. If the motion strip points right, this arrow goes left. Is left the side for this card?”

lessons.concept: Why force arrows name interactions on an object, not the direction it happens to move
